%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Understand how complex numbers are represented graphically, how to convert between rectangular and polar forms, and how to perform basic algebraic operations on them.

Section B.1-2 introduces the **algebra of complex numbers** — the rules and representations that make complex numbers useful as a mathematical tool. A complex number \(z = a + jb\) can be plotted as a point \((a, b)\) on the complex plane, giving it both a geometric and algebraic identity. You will learn two equivalent forms — **rectangular** \(a + jb\) and **polar** \(re^{j\theta}\) — and how to move between them. Mastering this section means you can manipulate complex numbers confidently in signal analysis, circuit problems, and any context where real-world problems are solved more efficiently by passing through the complex domain.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. B.2](/figures/page-005-fig__b_2-1.png)
*Fig. B.2 shows the complex plane with \(z = a + jb\) plotted as point \((a, b)\), its magnitude \(r\) and angle \(\theta\), and its conjugate \(z^* = a - jb\) reflected across the real axis — the single diagram that anchors every representation in this section.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Rectangular form \(z = a + jb\) and polar form \(z = re^{j\theta}\) are two descriptions of the same point on the complex plane — the conversion formulas connect them, and the common trap is confusing the imaginary part \(b\) with the imaginary term \(jb\).*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777215665305-5582.png)

## Core Idea

A complex number has **two equivalent forms** that describe the same point on the complex plane:

$$
z = a + jb \quad \text{(rectangular)} \qquad z = re^{j\theta} = r(\cos\theta + j\sin\theta) \quad \text{(polar)}
$$

In rectangular form, \(a = \operatorname{Re}(z)\) is the **real part** and \(b = \operatorname{Im}(z)\) is the **imaginary part** — both are ordinary real numbers. In polar form, \(r = |z| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\) is the **magnitude** and \(\theta = \angle z = \arctan(b/a)\) is the **angle** (phase).

Conversion goes both ways:
$$
a = r\cos\theta, \quad b = r\sin\theta \qquad \Longleftrightarrow \qquad r = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}, \quad \theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

**Example:** For \(z = 3 + j4\), we get \(r = \sqrt{9+16} = 5\) and \(\theta = \arctan(4/3) \approx 53.1^\circ\), so \(z = 5e^{j0.927}\).

### COMMON EXAM TRAP

The **imaginary part** of \(z = a + jb\) is the scalar \(b\), **not** the term \(jb\). Writing \(\operatorname{Im}(z) = jb\) is a classic error that costs marks.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- A complex number \(z = a + jb\) is a point on the 2D complex plane with real part \(a\) and imaginary part \(b\).
- Polar form \(z = re^{j\theta}\) uses magnitude \(r\) and angle \(\theta\) to describe the same point.
- The imaginary part is the scalar \(b\), never \(jb\) — confusing the two is the most common exam trap.

*In the next section we will use these two forms to perform multiplication and division of complex numbers, where polar form reveals its true power: multiplication becomes addition of angles.*
